frontmatter.title: Cryptographic Algorithms and Key Sizes for Personal Identity Verification (Working Draft)
frontmatter.short_title: 800-78- Cryptographic Algorithms and Key Sizes for PIV
frontmatter.subtitle: Development Version for PQC

affiliations.name: National Institute of Standards and Technology
affiliations.department: Computer Security Division, Information Technology Lab

children.value: Federal Information Processing Standard 201-3 (FIPS 201-3) defines the requirements for Personal Identity Verification (
) life cycle activities, including identity proofing, registration,

Card usage. FIPS 201-3 also defines the structure of an identity credential that includes cryptographic keys. This document contains the technical specifications needed for the mandatory and optional cryptographic keys specified in FIPS 201-3, as well as the supporting infrastructure specified in FIPS 201-3 and the related NIST Special Publication (SP) 800-73, Interfaces for Personal Identity Verification, and SP 800-76, Biometric Specifications for Personal Identity Verification, which rely on cryptographic functions.
This page contains a working draft of SP 800-78-6,
Cryptographic Algorithms and Key Sizes for Personal Identity Verification,
as it is being updated to support post-quantum cryptography. The current, final version of this publication, SP 800-78-5, is available

Homeland Security Presidential Directive-12 (HSPD-12) mandated the creation of new standards for interoperable identity credentials for physical and logical access to Federal
Government locations and systems. Federal Information Processing Standard 201 (FIPS 201), Personal Identity Verification (
) of Federal Employees and Contractors, was developed to establish standards for identity credentials
National Institute of Standards and Technology, 2022
. This document, NIST Special Publication (SP) 800-78-5, specifies the cryptographic algorithms and key sizes for
systems and is a companion document to FIPS 201-3.
The scope of this recommendation encompasses the

Card, infrastructure components that
support issuance and management of the

Card, and applications that rely on the credentials
supported by the

Card to provide security services. This recommendation identifies
acceptable symmetric and asymmetric encryption algorithms, digital signature algorithms, key
establishment schemes, and message digest algorithms and specifies mechanisms to identify
the algorithms associated with

Algorithms and key sizes have been selected for consistency with applicable federal standards
and to ensure adequate cryptographic strength for
This document is intended for federal agencies and implementers of

systems. Readers are
assumed to have a working knowledge of cryptography and public key infrastructure (
The document is organized as follows:
, Introduction, provides the purpose, scope, audience, and assumptions of the document and outlines its structure
, Application of Cryptography in FIPS 201-3, identifies the cryptographic mechanisms and objects that employ cryptography, as specified in FIPS 201-3 and its supporting documents.
, On-Card Cryptographic Requirements, describes the cryptographic requirements for cryptographic keys and authentication information stored on the
, Certificate Status Information, describes the cryptographic requirements for status information generated by
Card Application Administration Keys, describes the cryptographic requirements for managing information stored on the
Card Interfaces, specifies key reference values and algorithm identifiers for the application programming interface and card commands defined in
National Institute of Standards and Technology, 2024
, Cryptographic Algorithm Validation Testing Requirements, specifies the cryptographic algorithm validation testing that must be performed on the
Card based on the keys and algorithms that it supports.
contains the list of documents used as references in this recommendation.
, List of Symbols, Abbreviations, and Acronyms, contains the list of acronyms used in this document.
, Change Log, describes the changes made to SP 800-78 since its initial release.

FIPS 201-3 employs cryptographic mechanisms to authenticate cardholders, secure information
stored on the

Card, and secure the supporting infrastructure. FIPS 201-3 and its supporting
documents specify a suite of keys to be stored on the

Card for personal identity verification,
digital signature generation, and key management. The

cryptographic keys specified in FIPS
201-3 and SP 800-73 are:
An asymmetric digital signature key for signing documents and messages,
An asymmetric key management key that supports key establishment or key transport and up to 20 retired key management keys,
Secure Messaging key that supports the establishment of session keys for use with secure messaging and cardholder authentication using the SM-AUTH authentication mechanism as defined in
National Institute of Standards and Technology, 2024
The cryptographic algorithms, key sizes, and parameters that may be used for these keys are specified in
Cards must implement private key computations for one or more of the algorithms identified in this section.
Cryptographically protected objects specified in FIPS 201-3, SP 800-73, and SP 800-76 include:
The X.509 certificates for each asymmetric key on the
A secure messaging card verifiable certificate (
for the public key needed to verify the signature on the secure messaging
A digitally signed Card Holder Unique Identifier (
Digitally signed biometrics using the Common Biometric Exchange Formats Framework (
The SP 800-73 Security Object, which is a digitally signed hash table.
specifies the cryptographic algorithms, key sizes, and parameters that may be used to protect these objects. Certification authorities (
) and card management systems that protect these objects must support one or more of the cryptographic algorithms, key sizes, and parameters specified in
Applications may be designed to use any or all of the cryptographic keys and objects stored on the
Card. Where maximum interoperability is required, applications should support all of the identified algorithms, key sizes, and parameters specified in
s and Online Certificate Status Protocol (
) responders to generate and distribute digitally signed certificate revocation lists (
status messages, respectively. These certificate status mechanisms support validation of the
cardholder, the cardholder’s digital signature key, and the cardholder’s key management key.
The signed certificate status mechanisms specified in FIPS 201-3 are:
s that specify the status of a group of X.509 certificates and
status response messages that specify the status of a particular X.509 certificate.
The cryptographic algorithms, key sizes, and parameters that may be used to sign these mechanisms are specified in
, which also describes rules for encoding the signatures to ensure interoperability.
FIPS 201-3 permits optional card management operations. These operations may only be performed after the
Card authenticates the card management system. Card management systems are authenticated through the use of
Card Application Administration Keys. The cryptographic algorithms and key sizes that may be used for these keys are specified in
FIPS 201-3 identifies a suite of objects that are stored on the
Card for use in authentication mechanisms or other security protocols. These objects may be divided into three classes: cryptographic keys, signed authentication information stored on the
Card, and message digests of information stored on the
. Cryptographic requirements for other stored objects are detailed in
FIPS 201-3 and SP 800-73 specify six types of cryptographic keys to be used as credentials by the
The mandatory asymmetric Card Authentication key,
An optional symmetric Card Authentication key (deprecated),
A conditionally mandatory digital signature key,
A conditionally mandatory key management key,
An optional asymmetric key to establish session keys for secure messaging and to authenticate the cardholder using the SM-AUTH authentication mechanism.
All cryptographic algorithms employed shall provide at least 112 bits of security strength. Cryptographic keys that will remain in use after 2030 should provide 128 bits of security strength.

Federal departments and agencies should consider potential cryptographic key length migrations as part of their moderate to long-term cryptographic transition and modernization plans, including the need to plan and invest for a future migration to post-quantum algorithms. Capital investments for

issuance and relying party systems should be selected with an emphasis on ensuring a timely migration to post-quantum algorithms once standards, technologies, and services are available. If a migration to longer cryptographic keys would require significant resources or infrastructure upgrades, federal departments and agencies may elect to defer these improvements until the post-quantum migration. Post-quantum algorithms will be specified in a future revision of this document once foundational standards supporting their use have been adopted.
establishes specific requirements for cryptographic algorithms and key sizes for each key type.

children.value: In addition to the key sizes, keys must be generated using secure parameters. Rivest-Shamir-Adleman (
) keys must be generated using a public exponent of 65537. Elliptic curve keys must correspond to one of the following recommended curves from
National Institute of Standards and Technology, 2023
Elliptic curve keys are a faster option than
-based keys for the Card Authentication key for physical access since elliptic curve private key computation time is significantly shorter than
-based private key computation time. There is no phaseout date specified for either curve.
Card Application supports the virtual contact interface
National Institute of Standards and Technology, 2024
and the digital signature key, the key management key, or any of the retired key management keys are elliptic curve keys that correspond to Curve P-384, then the
Secure Messaging key shall use P-384. Otherwise, it may use P-256 or P-384.
While this specification requires that the
keys be 65537, applications should be able to process
public keys that have any public exponent that is an odd positive integer greater than or equal to 65537 and less than 2256.
This specification requires the key management key to be an
key transport key or an Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman (
National Institute of Standards and Technology, 2019
National Institute of Standards and Technology, 2018
Specification of Digital Signatures on Authentication Information

children.label: Specification of Digital Signatures on Authentication Information

children.value: FIPS 201-3 requires the use of digital signatures to protect the integrity and authenticity of information stored on the
Card. FIPS 201-3 and SP 800-73 require digital signatures on the following objects stored on the
The optional secure messaging card verifiable certificate (
Approved digital signature algorithms are specified in
National Institute of Standards and Technology, 2023
provides specific requirements for public key algorithms as well as key sizes, hash algorithms, and padding schemes for generating digital signatures for digitally signed information stored on the
Card. Agencies are cautioned that generating digital signatures with elliptic curve algorithms may initially limit interoperability.
ML-DSA parameter sets will need be selected and added to the signature algorithm table.

Specification of Public Keys in X.509 Certificates

children.label: Specification of Public Keys in X.509 Certificates

children.value: FIPS 201-3 requires the generation and storage of an X.509 certificate to correspond with each asymmetric private key contained on the
Secure Messaging key. X.509 certificates include object identifiers to specify the cryptographic algorithm associated with a public key.
specifies the object identifiers that may be used in certificates to indicate the algorithm for a subject public key.

Specification of Message Digests in the SP 800-73-4 Security Object

children.label: Specification of Message Digests in the SP 800-73-4 Security Object

children.value: SP 800-73 mandates the inclusion of a Security Object consistent with the Authenticity/Integrity Code defined by the International Civil Aviation Organization (
) in [MRTD]. This object contains message digests of other digital information stored on the

Card and is digitally signed. This specification requires that the message digests of digital information be computed using the same hash algorithm used to generate the digital signature on the Security Object. The set of acceptable algorithms is specified in
. The Security Object format identifies the hash algorithm used when computing the message digests by including an object identifier. The appropriate object identifiers are identified in
id-sha256 ::= {joint-iso-itu-t(2) country(16) us(840) organization(1) gov(101) csor(3) nistalgorithm(4) hashalgs(2) 1}
id-sha384 ::= {joint-iso-itu-t(2) country(16) us(840) organization(1) gov(101) csor(3) nistalgorithm(4) hashalgs(2) 2}
certificates transition to ML-DSA signatures, the
signing infrastructure must also support ML-DSA.
Card Issuance and Management Subsystem generates and distributes status information for
Secure Messaging keys. FIPS 201-2 mandates two formats for certificate status information:
status responses shall be digitally signed to support authentication and integrity using a key size and hash algorithm that satisfy the requirements for signing
, and that are at least as large as the key size and hash algorithm used to sign the certificate.
messages rely on object identifiers to specify which signature algorithm was used to generate the digital signature. The object identifiers specified in
messages to identify the signature algorithm.
Cards may support card activation by the card management system to support card personalization and post-issuance card updates.
Cards that support card personalization and post-issuance updates perform a challenge-response protocol using a symmetric cryptographic key (i.e., the
Card Application Administration Key) to authenticate the card management system. After successful authentication, the card management system can modify information stored on the

Algorithm and key size requirements for

children.title: Three key TDEA (TDEA with Keying Option 1)

children.value: SP 800-73 defines an application programming interface, the
Client Application Programming Interface (Part 3), and a set of mandatory card commands, the
Card Application Card Command Interface (Part 2). The command syntaxes for these interfaces identify
keys using one-byte key references, and their associated algorithms (or suites of algorithms) are specified using one-byte algorithm identifiers. The same identifiers are used in both interfaces.
specifies the key reference values for each of the
defines algorithm identifiers for each cryptographic algorithm supported by this specification.
identifies valid combinations of key reference values and algorithm identifiers.
Card key reference is a one-byte identifier that specifies a cryptographic key according to its
defines the key reference values used on the

Card algorithm identifier is a one-byte identifier that specifies a cryptographic algorithm and key size or a suite of algorithms and key sizes. For symmetric cryptographic operations, the algorithm identifier also specifies a mode of operation (i.e.,
). Table 9 lists the algorithm identifiers for the cryptographic algorithms that may be recognized on the
interfaces. All other algorithm identifier values are reserved for future use.
with identifier ‘00’ and ‘03’ is deprecated and will be removed in the next revision of this document.

Algorithm identifiers ‘27’ and ‘2E’ represent suites of algorithms and key sizes for use with secure messaging and key establishment. Cipher Suite 2 (CS2) is used to establish session keys and for secure messaging when the
(Curve P-256) key. Cipher Suite 7 (CS7) is used to establish session keys and for secure messaging when the
(Curve P-384) key. Details of secure messaging, the key establishment protocol, and the algorithms and key sizes for these two cipher suites are specified in SP 800-73-4, Part 2
National Institute of Standards and Technology, 2024
summarizes the set of algorithms supported for each key reference value.
All cryptographic algorithms employed shall provide at least 112 bits of security strength. Cryptographic keys that will remain in use after 2030 should provide 128 bits of security strength.

Federal departments and agencies should consider potential cryptographic key length migrations as part of their moderate to long-term cryptographic transition and modernization plans, including the need to plan and invest for a future migration to post-quantum algorithms. Capital investments for

issuance and relying party systems should be selected with an emphasis on ensuring a timely migration to post-quantum algorithms once standards, technologies, and services are available. If a migration to longer cryptographic keys would require significant resources or infrastructure upgrades, federal departments and agencies may elect to defer these improvements until the post-quantum migration. Post-quantum algorithms will be specified in a future revision of this document once foundational standards supporting their use have been adopted.
PQC: Algorithm Identifiers and Key References

New one-byte algorithm identifier values must be allocated for ML-DSA and ML-KEM variants. These must align across SP 800-78, SP 800-73 Parts 1 and 2, and any other specifications that reference them. New key reference values will also be needed for PQC variants of some key types.

) tests that are required for each supported key and algorithm at the time of publication.

This appendix is informative and provides an overview of the changes made to SP 800-78 since its initial release.
In August 2007, Revision 1 enhanced alignment with the National Security Agency’s Suite B Cryptography by:
Reducing the set of elliptic curves approved for use with
cards from six curves to two,
Realigning with the NSA Suite B Cryptographic specification by removing discontinued Elliptic Curve MQV as a key agreement scheme,
4096 as an algorithm and key size for generating signatures for
Eliminating the redundant cipher block chaining (
) mode of encryption for symmetric authentication purposes (challenge and response)
public key exponents with FIPS 186-3 and
-1 after December 31, 2010, when signing revocation information under limited circumstances.
Adding algorithm and key size requirements for secure messaging,
public keys may only have a public exponent of 65537.
In 2024, Revision 5 updates incorporated the following changes:

Table 1 reflects additional higher strength keys with at least 128-bit security and suggested sunsets of lower sized keys by 2030 in anticipation of the recommended migration to 128-bit security strength in 2031,
Accommodation of the Secure Messaging Authentication key,
Deprecation of the symmetric card authentication key,
